Please describe your research career to date
As the lead Senior Specialist Dietitian in one of only five nationally commissioned adult inherited metabolic
diseases (IMDs) services in England, I manage over 1000 patients with complex nutritional needs, including
phenylketonuria (PKU). Due to advances in neonatal screening and paediatric management, the number of
patients transitioning to adult services has increased exponentially. Despite diet being the mainstay of treatment,
there is limited evidence informing the dietary management of adults with PKU, and other IMDs, creating an urgent
need for research that was rarely previously needed, a novel gap that I aim to fill. I aspire to become a clinical
academic and research leader, optimising the health outcomes of adults living with

secarb__001_b.text:
IMDs.
Research experience
Pre-doctoral Clinical Academic Fellowship (PCAF)
I was awarded a HEE/NIHR PCAF in 2019 that afforded me the opportunity to extend my research knowledge and
skills. I completed a PGCert in Clinical Research, including quantitative and qualitative research design, data
analysis and critical appraisal, and during this PCAF, published 4 first-author peer-reviewed full publications,
totaling 10 full publications in my NHS career (see ‘Publication Record’). I expanded my research skills by
undertaking the following initiatives:
Evidence review and synthesis
I completed the “Cochrane: Conducting an Intervention Review” online course, and attended several courses at
King’s College London (KCL) on systematic searches and writing literature reviews. I consolidated these skills
when I led on a narrative review and scoping review on protein requirements and protein status in PKU, both
Dietetics1,2.
published in “Clinical Nutrition” the highest impact journal in Nutrition & My reviews are cited in multiple
publications including two consensus papers in PKU3,4.
Research design and analysis
I completed an online course in Logistic Regression and attended courses on resea

secarb__001_c.text:
rch development and analysis
(Good Clinical Practice, academic writing and SPSS data analysis).
I employed these skills when conducting a national survey to investigate knowledge, perceptions and dietary
behaviours of adults with PKU, published in the Journal of Human Nutrition & Dietetics5. I led on the
conceptualisation, design, ethics approval, questionnaire development in consultation with service users, analysis,
and publication.
Clinical research involvement
The Adult IMD service at Guy’s and St Thomas’ Hospital (GSTT) has a longstanding commitment to research,
collaborating with industry and other IMD services. I was the lead Dietitian for the following studies:
 Multi-centre trial of a new PKU supplement (Nutricia, 2017-2018).
Role: Recruitment, randomisation, data collection, data interpretation, and contribution to publication (see
‘Publication Record’).
 Multi-centre retrospective study led by Professor Anita MacDonald, Birmingham Children’s Hospital,
investigating metabolic control of patients with PKU across Europe and the UK (2019-2022).
Role: Recruitment, data interpretation and I will co-author prospective publications.
Supporting research within my department and

secarb__001_d.text:
discipline
I am passionate about Dietitians as leaders in clinical and research activities. I led on the design and delivery of
several audits and service evaluations. Over the last five years, the IMD Dietetic service has presented 14
abstracts at prestigious Metabolic conferences, of which I was first author or supervised a team member to lead on
the abstract. Undertaking my PhD will enable me to further support dietitians and other clinical academic
researchers.
Conference presentations
I have experience in presenting research abstracts at national and international conferences, including the
International Congress of Inborn Errors of Metabolism 2021 (Sydney, Australia), Society for the Study of Inborn
Errors of Metabolism (SSIEM) 2019 (Rotterdam, Netherlands), National Society of PKU conference 2019 (UK),
and British Inherited Metabolic Diseases conference 2018 and 2022 (UK), and have three abstracts submitted to
present at SSIEM 2024 (Porto). I was a panelist at the UK and Ireland Metabolic Dietitians meeting in 2023
contributing to discussions on protein requirements in IMDs, and have been invited to open this event in 2024,
presenting on my work in protein requirements and

secarb__001_e.text:
protein status in PKU.
Patient and public involvement and engagement (PPIE)
I established the first PKU PPIE group at GSTT in 2020 and have facilitated multiple meetings. This group has
been pivotal in the development of my doctoral research proposal (see ‘Patient and Public Involvement’). I have
undertaken training in PPIE, and aim to do further training in my PhD fellowship.
MSc Research Thesis
I completed my Masters (Distinction) in 2012 where my thesis assessed the feasibility of computer-tailored
nutrition communications in primary care. I was responsible for recruitment, conducting semi-structured interviews
with patients and healthcare professionals, and quantitative and qualitative data analysis.
Professional experience
Clinical skills and leadership
I am passionate about our NHS and developing our services, having worked in the NHS for eight years. As the
lead Senior Metabolic Dietitian at GSTT, I provide supervision and clinical leadership to the junior Dietitians. I
started in 2017 as the only Dietitian in the IMD service, and over the last seven years I have developed the service
to a team of four Dietitians and one Dietetic Assistant. I have led on multiple service dev

secarb__001_f.text:
elopment projects
including creating standardised care pathways, and advancing the role of Dietitians by establishing the first
Dietitian-led IMD clinics under GSTT, and developing training competencies and clinical resources to support
Dietitians managing complex IMD.
I am a visiting lecturer at KCL, teaching on the Advanced Diet Therapy training module for dietetic students.
National and international advisor
I have been involved in the development and publication of national consensus guidelines for PKU and other IMD
conditions, either as the lead contributor from GSTT or supervising a member of staff to support their involvement
and contribution to the publications3,6,7,8. I am a co-author of the IMD chapter in the Manual of Dietetic Practice (7th
Edition, in press).
GSTT is a centre of excellence for a rare condition, Adult Refsum Disease (ARD). I am the international lead
Dietitian for ARD, developing the international dietary guidelines and providing clinical expertise to Dietitians
worldwide, and sit on the Advisory Board for the Global DARE Foundation.
Membership
As a member of the British Metabolic Dietitians Group, I have been actively involved in advancing clinical prac

in IMD through contributing to the development of consensus statements and care pathways.

This application, submitted in 2022, has been revised based on the feedback received. I scored highly for all the
‘Applicant’ sections (both reviewers providing an overall score of 5, excellent). Reviewers commented on the
strength of the Patient and Public Involvement and Engagement (PPIE), but with a suggestion to increase PPIE
members in the Project Advisory Group from one to 2-3 members. The supervisory team and host organisation
were very highly regarded by the reviewers; however, there was recommendation to consider a further practice
supervisor of the same professional background. The following feedback was provided for the research plan:
greater consideration for the design of the intervention in r

secarb__001_h.text:
elation to the MRC framework; incorporating further
qualitative research to inform intervention development and delivery from both the participants’ and healthcare
professionals’ (HCP) perspectives, and determining how exercise intervention fidelity would be assessed. With
recommendations to include more qualitative research, further training on qualitative research was indicated.
This application differs from previous submissions in that the following amendments have been made in response
to the above feedback:
1. Project Advisory Group: Three PPIE members will now be in the Advisory Group. A diverse PPIE will be
selected to ensure members represent the views of the wider PKU community.
2. Practice supervisors: Dr Danielle Bear has joined my supervisory team as a member from the same
professional background as myself. Dr Bear is a former NIHR ICA awardee and since completing her
NIHR PhD has been appointed as a Consultant Dietitian (GSTT) and Clinical Academic Lecturer in
Nutritional Sciences (KCL) (see ‘Supervision’ section).
3. Research plan:
a. MRC/NIHR and TFA frameworks - The MRC and NIHR framework informed the research
proposal including incorporating the six core elements i

n the development and assessment of the
intervention. Additionally, the Theoretical Framework of Acceptability (TFA) will now inform
assessments of intervention acceptability. The initial study included establishing patients’
acceptability of the intervention; however, the perspective of the HCP was not included. The
revised research plan includes a focus group with Dietitians to include their perspectives.
b. Fidelity of physiotherapy intervention - Collaborations with the physiotherapist has
strengthened exercise intervention fidelity, with the rehabilitation treatment specification system
informing the development of the intervention (see ‘Research plan’). Each participant will receive

allocation of participants. Throughout the 12-week intervention period, exercise type, frequency
and intensity will be assessed to monitor adherence to the protocol.

secpesor__001_a.text:
Phenylketonuria (PKU) is an inherited metabolic condition that leads to the buildup of an amino acid (a building
block of protein) called phenylalanine. All babies born in the UK are tested for PKU at birth. High phenylalanine
levels can cause severe brain damage; however, early treatment prevents this. Treatment is for life, and involves
reducing the amount of protein in the diet (to usually less than 15 grams daily) to limit phenylalanine intake. The
treatment also involves taking medical supplements (called PKU supplements) that contain all amino acids, except
for phenylalanine.
For adults with PKU, we do not know the ideal amount of protein needed each day (from foods and PKU
supplements). We know from studies in healthy adults that dietary protein plays an important role in keeping
muscles healthy. It is important to get the amount of protein right for adults with PKU. Too little protein could lead
to muscle problems later in life, whereas too much protein, through taking more PKU supplements, could make the
diet too hard to follow.
We have planned two studies:
Study 1: Do people with PKU suffer from poor muscle health, and is it related to diet?
We will measure how a PKU diet
secpesor__001_a.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Plain English Summary of Research

secpesor__001_b.text:
affects muscle mass (the amount of muscle someone has) and muscle function
(how well the muscle works) in adults with PKU. Participants will be asked to keep a record of their food intake for
four days, and to wear an accelerometer on their thigh, a match box size device that monitors movement, for a
week before visiting a centre for the following tests:
1. Exercises to measure muscle function: jumping, climbing steps and grip strength
2. Muscle mass measurements: a DXA scan (full-body x-ray scan), bioelectrical impedance analysis (similar
to scans used in some gyms, four small stickers are placed on the hands and feet), and skin thickness in
different places on the body using a hand-held device (skinfold caliper)
3. Blood tests to measure levels of different proteins
4. A bloodspot test to measure phenylalanine (similar to a finger prick test for blood sugar)
5. Questionnaires about diet, quality of life, and exercise
Study 2: Does increasing protein intake improve muscle mass and function in PKU?
Participants in Study 1 who are found to have a low muscle function will be invited to join a second study. This
study will see if taking more protein over 12 weeks improves muscle mass
secpesor__001_b.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Plain English Summary of Research

secpesor__001_c.text:
and/or function. Half the participants will
take their normal amount of protein, and the other half will be asked to take more protein (through increased PKU
supplements). Everyone will be advised to do regular exercises.
During the 12 weeks, participants will keep a diary to record their diet and exercise routine. They will be asked to
send in a bloodspot test every 14 days, similar to their current clinical care.
After 12 weeks, the participants will repeat the same tests as Study 1. They will also have an ultrasound scan to
measure the size of their arm and thigh muscles at the beginning and end of Study 2.
Finally, participants and Dietitians will be asked to give feedback on the studies to help improve further research.
People with PKU have been involved in the study design and will continue to help with patient information leaflets,
questionnaires, and sharing the results with the PKU community.
We hope that these studies will help design a larger study looking at how diet can be adjusted to improve muscle
mass and function in adults with PKU. The results will also guide Doctors and Dietitians to ensure adults with PKU
maintain good health as they age.
secpesor__001_c.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Plain English Summary of Research

secsa__001_a.text:
Background
Phenylketonuria (PKU) is an inherited metabolic disorder of protein metabolism. Treatment involves limiting dietary
protein to restrict phenylalanine intake and supplementation with protein substitutes providing all other amino
acids. The optimal protein intake for adults with PKU is unknown. Current protein intake guidelines are
extrapolated from healthy populations, using estimates based on nitrogen balance (NB) methodology, which have
limitations in both healthy adults and in PKU. Protein requirements based on Indicator Amino Acid Oxidation
(IAAO) are up to 55% higher for PKU. Establishing optimal protein intake in adults with PKU is essential as
inadequate protein intake will likely compromise muscle mass and function, whereas excessive protein intake,
through increased protein substitute doses, is a substantial burden to patients.
I will conduct two studies with the following aims and methods:
Study 1: Observational study
The aim will be to assess muscle mass and function of adults with PKU, and investigate whether impairments are
associated with current dietary management practices.
A single-centre observational study will recruit 50 participants with PKU, aged ≥18

secsa__001_b.text:
years following a stable
management plan. Participants will complete a 4-day food diary and wear an accelerometer for 7 days prior to
attending a test day. A range of gold standard and common clinical assessments of muscle mass and function with
be conducted, including: handgrip strength, isokinetic dynamometry, 6-minute step test, vertical jump test, dual
energy X-ray absorptiometry, bioelectrical impedance analysis, skinfold thickness, and various biochemical
markers of protein status. All measurements will be compared to normative data. Dietary intake, physical activity
and quality of life will also be assessed.
Participants on a PKU diet with a handgrip strength <25th centile of reference data will be invited to participate in
Study 2.
Study 2: Feasibility study
The aim will be to examine the feasibility of a study investigating the impact of prescribing protein intake, based on
NB-informed protein requirements (current practice) versus IAAO-informed protein requirements, on muscle mass
and function.
A randomised control trial (RCT) feasibility study will recruit 24 participants. Eligible participants will be randomised
into one of two arms for 12-weeks:
 NB protein requireme

secsa__001_c.text:
nts (0.83g/kg/day) plus home-based exercise programme
 IAAO protein requirements (1.2g/kg/day) plus home-based exercise programme
Assessment data from Study 1 will form baseline measurements for Study 2, and will be repeated at week 12.
Additionally, muscle mass and thickness will be measured by upper and lower limb ultrasound at weeks 0 and 12.
During the 12-weeks, participants will complete fortnightly bloodspots and physical activity questionnaires.
Adherence to the interventions will be monitored through a weekly log. Feasibility measures, including recruitment,
retention and intervention fidelity will be collected, and questionnaires and semi-structured interviews conducted to
evaluate intervention acceptability.
Impact and dissemination
This research will contribute to the development of evidence-based dietary guidelines for adults with PKU.
Findings will be published in peer-reviewed journals and presented at conferences. Study 2 will provide preliminary
data to inform the design of a full-scale RCT investigating dietary and lifestyle factors modulating muscle mass and
function in adults with PKU.

secdrp__001_a.text:
Background
Phenylketonuria (PKU) is a rare inherited metabolic disorder of protein metabolism with a prevalence of 1:10,000
in the UK. Left untreated, high serum phenylalanine causes significant irreversible neurocognitive disability.
Dietary management remains the mainstay of treatment, consisting of lifelong adherence to a very low protein diet
(typically <15 grams per day) and provision of up to 80% of protein intake from phenylalanine-free or low-
phenylalanine protein substitutes9.
1. Problems being addressed
Establishing optimal protein intake in adults with PKU: “Not too little, not too much”
With the onset of newborn screening for PKU in 1969, there is now an increasing adult and ageing PKU
population requiring dietary guidance to support changing metabolic demands across the lifespan. In my narrative
review, I highlighted that there is currently limited evidence informing protein requirement guidelines for adults
with PKU1.
Current protein intake guidance for adults with PKU is extrapolated from healthy population data, using estimates
of protein requirements based on either nitrogen balance (NB) methodology or from younger adults with PKU1.
The limitations associated with

secdrp__001_b.text:
NB methodology are widely recognised10. Utilising Indicator Amino Acid Oxidation
(IAAO) as a more contemporary approach to estimating protein requirements, protein requirements have been
reported to be 39-55% higher in PKU, than recommendations informed by NB methodology11. Increased
consumption of specialised protein substitutes would be required to achieve this higher protein intake; however,
people on a PKU diet report poor palatability of these supplements, and adherence to a PKU diet has been shown
to reduce with age12-14.
The impact of the PKU diet on muscle mass and function, and by extension, risk of cardiometabolic disease and
frailty in later life, is unknown2,15. Given that a PKU diet involves modifying sources of ingested protein,
establishing the optimal protein intake in adults with PKU is essential because an inadequate protein intake will
likely compromise muscle mass and function (encompassing strength, power and mobility), whereas excessive
protein intake will result in a substantial prescribed supplement burden to patients.
2. Importance of research to improving health of service users and health care services in PKU
Adults with PKU are concerned about their mus

secdrp__001_c.text:
cle mass and function and impact on quality of life
Our PKU PPIE group identified that adults with PKU are concerned about the impact of the PKU diet on functional
ability as they age. My nationwide questionnaire survey in 137 people with PKU identified that >50% have
concerns for their long-term health, echoing the issues raised by our PPIE group5. Maintenance of muscle mass is
crucial for supporting function, reducing cardiometabolic risk and frailty, and improving quality of life (QoL) with
advancing age16.
Following a very restrictive diet is a significant burden for people with PKU. In the UK, 73% of adults find PKU
dietary management difficult and 39% do not take their protein substitutes as prescribed12. Poor dietary
adherence has been reported to increase guilt and anxiety, thus negatively impacting health13. Research is
therefore needed to establish the minimum dietary protein intake that translates to optimal muscle mass and
function, without compromising phenylalanine control, to support healthy ageing in PKU.
Research is urgently needed to inform patient care
I surveyed Dietitians from nine UK-based metabolic centres and found varying protein prescribing practices. The

secdrp__001_d.text:
UK Metabolic Dietitians Annual meeting 2023 included a discussion on ‘Protein requirements – have we got it
right?’, for which I was a panelist. Concerns were raised regarding discrepancies in dietary management as a
result of limited evidence. Current European PKU guidelines recommend the FAO/WHO/UNU safe minimum
studies9;
protein requirements informed by NB however, the recent IAAO informed protein requirements indicate
this significantly underestimates requirements11.
Current clinical practice is informed by guidelines without any scientific basis in the target patient group - to date
no studies have investigated the impact of protein intake informed by NB vs. IAAO on the important endpoints of
muscle mass and function in adults with PKU – and I want to change this.
With protein playing an essential role in skeletal muscle health17, there is an urgent unmet need for research to
better inform protein requirements for adults with PKU and how protein status is monitored in clinical practice.
3. Review of existing evidence
Timing
My narrative review highlighted a paucity of evidence regarding protein requirements for people with PKU, with
significant omissions in adult specific reco

secdrp__001_e.text:
mmendations1. There is an immediate need for research specifically for
adults with PKU who, following successful neonatal screening programmes, are for the first time now entering
middle-older adulthood, and are being advised lifelong PKU restricted diets. We must ensure dietary management
leads to optimal health outcomes across the life course.
Relevance
Protein status in adults with PKU
I designed and published the protocol18, and subsequently performed and published the first-ever scoping review
to understand how dietary management translates to body protein status (encompassing anthropometric,
biochemical and functional measures) in people with PKU2. I identified 70 studies that measured relevant
markers; however, only one study assessed protein status as the primary outcome. The majority of studies were
conducted in children and adolescents with PKU, with very few in adults. Taken together, this review highlights a
significant gap in the current evidence and urgent need for research focused on understanding protein status in
adults with PKU.
Relevant outcome measurements
In my scoping review, I identified a small number of studies with mixed findings regarding muscle mass in a

secdrp__001_f.text:
dults
with PKU vs. healthy controls2. The only study to measure muscle function reported 38% of participants with PKU
had grip strength <3rd percentile2,19. Furthermore, pre-albumin (biochemical marker of protein status) was lower in
people with PKU2. Since publishing my scoping review, two recent studies have reported lower muscle mass and
handgrip strength in adults with PKU compared to controls20,21; however, these studies were in a different
healthcare system (Chile and Hungary) and did not address the questions raised in this research proposal. To
date, no study has measured muscle mass, function and biochemical markers of protein status in adults with
PKU, and investigated impacts on QoL. This multiple measurement approach is important for two reasons; first,
because adequate muscle mass does not mean adequate muscle function22 and it is muscle function that is
important to people with PKU, and second, because Dietitians do not know the best measurement method to use
in practice.
Relevant intervention
The optimal protein intake for adults with PKU remains unknown. No intervention studies have investigated the
impact of recommending dietary protein intake based on NB-informed

secdrp__001_g.text:
protein requirements (0.83g/kg/day)
(standard current practice) vs. IAAO-informed protein requirements (1.2g/kg/day) on muscle mass and function in
adults with PKU. I aim to establish protein requirements for adults with PKU by performing a feasibility study (as
part of my PhD) and then undertaking a larger RCT (as part of a NIHR ACAF).
4. Research aims and objectives
This research proposal aims to:
(i) assess muscle mass, muscle function and biochemical parameters of protein status in adults with PKU;
(ii) investigate whether impairments in muscle mass, muscle function and biochemical parameters in adults with
PKU are associated with PKU dietary management practices;
(iii) examine the feasibility of a study investigating the comparison of protein intake informed by NB (0.83g/kg/day)
vs. IAAO (1.2g/kg/day) and its impact on muscle mass and function, and phenylalanine control in adults with PKU.
Objectives
I propose to undertake two studies:
Study 1: Observational study
 Primary objective: To investigate differences in muscle mass and function (anthropometric, functional and
biochemical measurements) in adults with PKU vs. reference healthy population dataset.
Secondary objective:

secdrp__001_h.text:
To investigate associations between dietary factors (protein intake, protein

substitute use), muscle mass and physical activity, and muscle function in adults with PKU.
Study 2: Feasibility study:
 To determine the impact of increasing prescribed protein intake and exercise training on muscle mass
and function in adults with PKU who have compromised muscle strength.
 To establish the feasibility of implementing this complex intervention over 12 weeks in adults with PKU,
with the view to designing a full-scale RCT.
5. Project Plan
Study 1: Observational study
Study design
A cross-sectional, observational study will be conducted over an 18-month period in adults with PKU enrolled from
the Adult Metabolic Service at GSTT.
An observational study is critical to understanding the association between current dietary management practices
on muscle mass and function in adults with PKU and to establish the most robust endpoints for use in future trials.
The proposed research will be the first to simultaneously measure anthropometric, functional and biochemical
parameters of protein status in an adult UK cohort with PKU.
Methods
Recruitment and consent
Fifty adults with PKU will be recrui

secdrp__001_i.text:
ted. Following advice from our PPIE group, due to the large geographical area
covered by the service, appointments to discuss the study will be virtual (video/telephone). Eligible patients will be
sent a study information pack and participant consent will be returned via post. Additionally, to ensure equality of
access to participation, travel and time taken away from work or other duties will be reimbursed.
Inclusion criteria:
 Diagnosed with PKU on newborn screening
 Followed a stable management plan for ≥12 months
 Aged ≥18 years
 Able to walk independently up at least 1 flight of stairs
Exclusion criteria:
 Plasma phenylalanine concentration <600 µmol/L without treatment (mild PKU)
 Pregnant, lactating or pre-conception (as dietary management and metabolic control differs)
 Other co-morbidities that could influence protein intake and digestion
Data collection
The protocol is outlined in Figure 1. Participants will attend a test day at the Centre for Human and Applied
Physiological Sciences, King’s College London (KCL) for ~3 hours. I will perform all assessments outlined below,
and data will be entered into REDCap EDC.
Muscle function
Muscle function assessments will be

secdrp__001_j.text:
the primary focus of this observational study as they were deemed most
important by our PPIE group. I have selected the following assessments with normative reference data that are
validated in physically active, adult populations23–26. With the exception of handgrip strength (measured in only
two PKU studies)19,21, none of these functional assessments have previously been measured in adults with PKU.
Handgrip strength – Widely recognised as a strong predictor of whole-body muscle strength27 and prognostic of
age-related health outcomes28.
Isokinetic dynamometry – This technique measures dynamic lower limb muscular contraction and is the gold
standard to assess muscle strength and power29.
Whilst isokinetic dynamometry is gold standard, it cannot be easily used in the clinical setting. Measurements by
isokinetic dynamometry will be compared to handgrip strength, a tool readily available in the clinical setting with
potential for widespread use in PKU following this study.
6-minute step test (6MST) - Exercise capacity is indirectly correlated with functional ability in healthy adults, and is
a key determinant of QoL and health outcomes30. The test will be performed on a single 20-cm

secdrp__001_k.text:
step in accordance
with the published protocol25.
Vertical jump test on force platform - The vertical jump test provides a measurement of lower limb muscular
power, an important physiological measure associated with the ability to perform activities of daily living and a
sensitive measurement of muscular change in healthy adults31. Vertical ground reaction force will also be
measured using a laboratory force plate, the gold standard for this measurement.
Anthropometry assessment
A range of gold standard and common clinical measures of anthropometry will be used. The correlation between
gold standard (e.g. dual energy X-ray absorptiometry (DXA)) vs. common clinical measures (e.g. bioelectrical
impedance analysis (BIA) and skinfolds) remain unknown in people with PKU, and can be measured here to
inform assessment approaches in clinical practice.
DXA - the gold standard measurement of lean mass, will be used to assess whole body and regional lower limb
lean mass. DXA measurements of lean mass closely correlate with measurements from MRI and CT, but at a
lower cost and with only small doses of radiation32.
BIA and skinfold thickness - these measurements provide estimates of fat-free m

secdrp__001_l.text:
ass and are common methods
for assessing body composition in a clinical setting. BIA has been validated in children and adolescents with
PKU32, but not adults2. Assessing fat-free mass in adults with PKU using these easily accessible techniques is
both practical and cost effective.
Height, weight, body mass index, and waist and thigh circumference will also be assessed.
Biochemical markers
Biochemical markers to assess protein status of people with PKU, including plasma amino acids, albumin,
prealbumin, total protein, retinol-binding protein and related biomarkers, will be measured. On test days, venous
blood samples (maximum of 20 ml) will be collected, processed, stored and analysed by the Clinical Biochemistry
laboratory at GSTT.
Metabolic control – Management of PKU in adults aims for blood phenylalanine concentrations between 120-600
µmol/L. Dried bloodspots (Guthrie cards) will be collected on waking on Day -7 and the test day, in line with
patients’ usual methods for self-monitoring.
Dietary assessment
Food diary and Food Frequency Questionnaire (FFQ)
A four-day food diary will be recorded and analysed by Nutritics software to determine current dietary protein
intake. In add

secdrp__001_m.text:
ition, the EPIC-FFQ, validated in adults within the UK34, will be completed to gain insight into
habitual intakes and compared to food diary data to identify whether FFQ is accurate to be routinely used in
practice.
Physical activity assessment
Assessments of physical activity in adults with PKU are limited2. A range of physical activity measures will be
recorded, including gold standard and clinical measures.
Accelerometery – Habitual activity will be recorded for 7 days prior to the test day using a thigh-worn activPAL™
accelerometer device, which our PPIE group regarded as feasible. This accelerometer quantifies free-living daily
activities, accurately recording the duration and intensity of all activities35.
International Physical Activity Questionnaire – Short Form (IPAQ-SF) - IPAQ-SF is a validated and widely used
self-report questionnaire providing internationally comparable data on physical activity36. This questionnaire will be
used alongside accelerometry to assess physical activity.
Health-related QoL
UK37.
The PKU-QoL questionnaire has been validated in adults with PKU in the This questionnaire will be used to
understand the impact of PKU on physical and mental health,

secdrp__001_n.text:
psychosocial factors, and the challenges and
barriers of adherence to a PKU-specific diet.
Other measurements – A questionnaire will be developed, and piloted with our PPIE group, to gather information
on diagnosis, PKU management (dietary and pharmacological) and adherence, and demographic characteristics
(e.g. age, sex, ethnicity) to ensure the study cohort is representative of the wider PKU population.
Data analysis
The following statistical analysis plan was developed in conjunction with KCL Statistical Support services. I will be
responsible for all data analysis with ongoing and extensive support from the KCL Statistical Services and my
supervisors.
Sample size
A sample size of 43 is required to detect differences in the primary measure (handgrip strength) in comparison to
healthy reference population data. This sample size is sufficient to detect a difference in mean grip force z-score
of -0.64 ± 1.26 (reported in Choukair et al, 2017) with 90% power and 5% significance from a two-sided one-
sample t-test (with the sample taken from a limited population of 4000 individuals with PKU in the UK).
Up to 50 participants will be recruited to obtain complete data for at least 43 pa

secdrp__001_o.text:
rticipants. This sample size will be
sufficient to investigate the association between handgrip strength and other continuous variables such as total
protein intake, muscle mass and physical activity. This sample size will achieve 83.5% power to detect moderate
correlation between variables (Pearson’s correlation coefficient >0.4) using a two-sided hypothesis test with 5%
significance.
Analysis
Participant characteristics and muscle mass and function measures will be reported using n (%) for categorical
data, and mean (±SD) or median (IQR) as appropriate for continuous data. Associations and differences between
handgrip strength (continuous variable) and dietary factors, muscle mass and physical activity will be analysed
using Pearson’s correlation coefficient for continuous variables, unpaired t-test for binary variables and one-way
ANOVA for polychotomous variables with Bonferroni post hoc correction. Variable selection methods will be
employed to select ~5 variables that explain the greatest variability in handgrip strength (or other outcomes of
interest) prior to the use of linear regression models. All analysis will be conducted using SPSS Statistics and
statistical significan

secdrp__001_p.text:
ce will be p<0.05.
Study 2: Feasibility study
Study design
The feasibility study will be a RCT with two intervention arms conducted over 12-weeks (see Figure 2). A 12-week
duration is frequently used in studies investigating the impact of protein intake and/or exercise training on muscle
mass and functional outcomes38.
The intervention is a complex intervention with the interacting components of diet and exercise, that are behaviour
interventions39
dependent. Therefore, the MRC/NIHR framework for complex guided the development of this
research proposal, along with involvement from our PPIE group. The validated Theoretical Framework of
Acceptability (TFA) will be used to inform assessments of intervention acceptability40.
The feasibility of conducting a large RCT to assess the impact of protein intake on muscle mass and function in
adults with PKU is unknown. A feasibility study will be important to ascertain the acceptability and practicality of
the interventions, willingness to participate, retention rate, and to inform intervention development and delivery
from both the patients’ and healthcare professionals’ perspectives.
It remains unclear which outcome(s) relating to muscle ma

secdrp__001_q.text:
ss and function will be the most appropriate in a large
clinical trial, and therefore, multiple gold standard (e.g. DXA and isokinetic dynamometry) and common clinical
outcomes (e.g. BIA, ultrasound and handgrip strength) will be conducted in this feasibility study.
An exercise intervention will be included in both study arms, as exercise, especially resistance-exercise training
(RET), has a fundamental role alongside dietary protein intake in improving muscle mass and function38.
Furthermore, our PPIE group felt it was important to offer exercise to both groups, and in doing so, reducing
confounding if habitual activity differed between groups.
Methods
Recruitment and consent
Eligible participants will be identified at the end of their observational study test day based on their handgrip
strength and dietary data. A handgrip strength <25th centile of normative reference data has been selected to
identify those who might benefit from optimising muscle function23. Participants will be provided with study
information and consent sought (with up to 1 week to consider participation).
Inclusion criteria:
Handgrip strength <25th centile

 Currently on a PKU diet
 Taking ≥75% of prescr

secdrp__001_r.text:
ibed protein substitutes weekly
Exclusion criteria:
Unable to take protein substitutes for an extended period of time

Randomisation and schedule of treatment
Participants will be randomised (with support from CTU) to either one of two intervention arms:
 NB protein requirements (standard care) plus home-based exercise programme
 IAAO protein requirements plus home-based exercise programme
Dietary intervention
Following randomisation, the participant’s dietary regimen will be discussed and a supply of additional protein
substitutes arranged for the study duration if required.
Exercise intervention
A 12-week evidence-based home RET programme41,42 will be developed by a physiotherapist (funded into this
application) using the rehabilitation treatment specification system (RTSS)43. Rehabilitation interventions in
research often lack specificity. The RTSS overcomes this by utilising a common language, improving research
reporting and allows the refinement of the intervention’s underlying treatment theories44. One designated
physiotherapist, blinded to dietary group allocation, will deliver the exercise intervention. Each participant will
receive written guidance, exercise bands, and

secdrp__001_s.text:
a one-to-one video induction session to ensure correct exercise
technique and intensity.
Monthly video drop-in sessions will be run to offer participants the opportunity to discuss any questions regarding
these interventions. There will be two independent sessions, one with the physiotherapist and one with myself, for
participants to drop-in as required. Running separate sessions will ensure the physiotherapist remains blinded to
the dietary intervention.
Data collection
All measurements taken on the test day of the observational study will form participants’ baseline measurements
for the feasibility study. During the feasibility study, participants will complete fortnightly dried bloodspots
(metabolic control) and the IPAQ-SF (physical activity status). Adherence to the dietary and exercise intervention
will be monitored through a weekly log, including the monitoring of exercise duration and intensity.
Participants will return to KCL for a second test day at the end of week 12. Financial compensation will be
provided. I will perform all assessments, and data collected will be entered into REDCap EDC.
Additional measurements
Ultrasound – Bicep, forearm and quadriceps muscle layer

secdrp__001_t.text:
thickness and rectus femoris cross-sectional area
(RF ) will be measured by ultrasound at Weeks 0 and 12. These measures of muscle mass closely correlate
CSA
with DXA45,46 and MRI47 and muscle function in chronic disease48. RF has demonstrated sensitivity in detecting
CSA
changes in muscle mass in response to resistance training in COPD49. Ultrasound is a novel technique to assess
muscle mass in people with PKU, and has advantages in being portable, low-cost and non-invasive, and would
also represent a significant extended scope of practice for myself as a Dietitian.
Feasibility measures
The following feasibility parameters will be assessed to inform the intervention development for the full-scale RCT,
planned for my post-doctoral research:
Recruitment, randomisation, and attrition – The number of adults with PKU that meet eligibility criteria, the
proportion of eligible willing to participate and be randomised, and the retention rate, will be recorded.
Acceptability and practicality of the interventions and measurements – The Acceptability of Intervention Measure
and Feasibility Intervention Measure (2-minute questionnaires)50 will be completed by participants at week 6 and
12. Fu

secdrp__001_u.text:
rthermore, semi-structured online/telephone interviews will be conducted using an interview guide based on
TFA at week 12, recorded digitally and transcribed by myself. Dietary and physical activity assessments will
evaluate whether participants can successfully implement and sustain the dietary regimes and RET programme.
The acceptability and practicality of implementing the interventions in a healthcare service will be explored further
through a focus group with Dietitians providing the dietary support to this patient group.
Outcome parameters: The sensitivity and specificity of common clinical measurements vs. gold standard
measurements will be established, including their mean (SD) of absolute and change values and anticipated
effect sizes; therefore, determining the measurements to be used in the full-scale trial and informing the sample
size calculation.
Data Analysis
Sample size
A formal power calculation is not required for a feasibility study. However, an adequate sample size will ensure
the above feasibility measures can be determined. Twelve participants per group have been previously
recommended for such studies51, and therefore this study will aim to recruit 24 partici

secdrp__001_v.text:
pants in total. With 38% of
adults with PKU previously found to have a grip strength <3rd percentile19, it is anticipated that 24 participants can
be recruited from Study 1 (n=50) with inclusion criteria of a grip strength <25th percentile.
Analysis
Effectiveness conclusions will not be drawn from this feasibility study, but exploratory comparative analysis will be
performed on endpoint data. Feasibility measures will be reported descriptively, including numbers eligible,
consented, randomised, completed, and also data completeness. All analysis will be conducted using SPSS
Statistics. All analyses will be performed on an intention-to-treat and per protocol basis. Analysis of the interviews
and focus group will use the five stage Framework Analysis methodology to deductively code and organise data
TFA40,52.

6. Dissemination, Outputs & Impact
Research outputs
 Conference presentation: I will submit research abstracts to relevant national and international
metabolic conferences.
 Publications: I plan to publish the findings of my research in high impact peer-reviewed journals. There
will be at least two publications, one from the observational study and one from th

secdrp__001_w.text:
e feasibility study. In
addition, there will be method development publications relating to improving clinical assessment in PKU
(e.g. US vs DXA, FFQ vs food diary). All will be published open access to ensure wide dissemination.
 Healthcare innovation: My research will help to establish reliable markers of protein status to
incorporate into the clinical assessment and monitoring of patients with PKU. This will advance current
clinical practice in determining dietary requirements and monitoring the outcomes of dietary interventions.
Research dissemination to patients and professionals
I am keen to disseminate my findings to the patients who may benefit from it. Our PPIE group will be actively
involved in disseminating this research. During the first year of my Fellowship, I will work with our PKU PPIE
group to develop plans for informing service users and other NHS services nationwide of the research outputs,
and engage funding bodies to support future research.
The National Society of PKU (NSPKU, patient support charity) are key stakeholders in disseminating research to
national and international PKU communities via their website, social media and annual conference. I will
collab

secdrp__001_x.text:
orate with NSPKU to disseminate my research via these platforms.
The BIMDG specialist group consists of clinicians, clinical service leads and national advisors. I plan to present
my research at BIMDG conferences and group meetings to inform and engage key stakeholders influencing NHS
service provision for patients with PKU.
Impact on the health system (guidelines)
I intend to contribute to the review and development of dietary management guidelines informing clinical practice
2017)9
in the UK and Europe, such as in the ESPKU guidelines (last published and the UK consensus on Dietetic
Management of Adults with PKU (published 2022)3. These guidelines are reviewed every 3-5 years. My research
will contribute to the evidence informing protein requirement guidelines, dietary interventions, and assessment
and monitoring of protein status in adults with PKU.
Further research funding
I plan to develop a proposal for post-doctoral research through the NIHR ACAF scheme to undertake a full-scale
complex intervention trial to investigate the impact of dietary regimens and lifestyle factors to optimise muscle
mass and function in adults with PKU. In addition, other NIHR and MRC research grants

secdrp__001_y.text:
will be highly relevant to
my research area and will be targeted for future research funding.
Barriers to further research
An ongoing barrier to research in PKU is subject numbers due to the rare nature of the condition. However, 25%
of patients within our service have PKU, and with our patient numbers doubling every 5 years, I anticipate there
will be adequate numbers in our service alone to enable a large RCT.
Research Impact
Short-term (within 3 years): I plan to establish new care pathways for PKU clinical assessment. Incorporating
novel anthropometric and functional assessments will extend the scope of dietetic practice and allow patients to
receive more individualised care.
Medium-term (within 3-5 years): Outcomes of my research will inform the development of guidelines (in dietary
assessment, monitoring and intervention) that will in turn inform care pathways and practice standards for PKU
services nationally and internationally. Therefore, clinical practice will evolve to ensure optimal health outcomes
for PKU patients as they age. Furthermore, the feasibility study will inform the design of a full-scale clinical trial to
further investigate diet and lifestyle factors that

secdrp__001_z.text:
modulate muscle mass and function in adults with PKU.
Long-term (within 5+ years): With the revised guidelines, dietary assessment and monitoring of muscle mass
and function will be incorporated into routine clinical practice. With standardised assessment and monitoring,
multi-centre data can be collated, providing novel insight into the impact of PKU on health outcomes with ageing.
Outcomes from the full-scale clinical trial will inform further research into dietary advances to support ageing well
with PKU.
7. Project Management
The Project Advisory Group will consist of myself, my academic and practice supervisors, physiotherapist and
PPIE members. The group will meet at the beginning, mid-point and end of each study to discuss study
implementation, recruitment, retention, any issues raised during the study and progression against the agreed
timetable. In addition, the six core elements of the MRC/NIHR framework will be considered by the group to
inform how the research should proceed39. These meetings will be via video. Financial reimbursement has been
budgeted for three PPIE members’ involvement in these meetings. A diverse PPIE will be selected to ensure
members represent the

secdrp__001_{.text:
views of the wider PKU community.
I will meet quarterly with KCL Finance for support with financial management of the Award.
8. Ethical considerations
The majority of measures and interventions used in this study are widely used in other areas of the NHS or in
research. However, I have worked with the PPIE group to identify whether specific aspects of the study may have
ethical concerns, and to discuss how to mitigate them, as follows:
i. Some participants may feel sensitive about the results of their body composition and functional
assessments and wish to have results withheld. This option will be offered to participants.
ii. The venous blood samples and bloodspots can cause brief pain. These tests are part of routine care in
PKU and will therefore be tolerated by most participants. Additional guidance on bloodspot technique will
be offered to participants.
iii. All biochemical results will be reviewed by Dr Ramachandran (Supervisor and Metabolic Service Lead
GSTT), with any abnormal results being reported and referred to appropriate services.
iv. Participants identified through the observational study who find dietary adherence challenging (i.e. taking
<75% of protein substitute

secdrp__001_|.text:
s), will be offered a clinic follow-up review with a Dietitian in the GSTT service
to support them.
Participants have the right to refuse participation or withdraw from the studies without giving reasons and without
prejudicing their ongoing care.
9. Success Criteria
Observational study: Complete data set from at least 50 eligible participants
Obtaining adequate numbers of eligible participants is achievable from within the patient cohort at GSTT.
However, the service covers a large geographic area and there is a risk that some participants may not wish to
travel to central London. To overcome this, compensation for travel will be provided and, if required, the test day
will be booked for the same day as the patient’s routine clinic appointment.
Feasibility study: Recruitment of 24 eligible participants and adequate retention to establish feasibility of
progression to full trial
The eligibility criteria for the feasibility study were selected to identify participants with compromised handgrip
strength. If <24 participants are eligible, this is an important feasibility outcome, and the handgrip strength cut-off
will be revised to ensure an adequate sample size for the feasibility st

secdrp__001_}.text:
udy.
Our PPIE group have provided strategies to support retention during the feasibility study, such as organising
virtual drop-in sessions.
A traffic light system, as recommended in best practice guidelines53, provides criteria that will be used to guide
progression of the feasibility study to a full-scale trial (see Table 1).
