Please describe how patients/service users, carers and the public have been involved in developing this
proposal
Phenylketonuria PPIE group
I founded the first Phenylketonuria (PKU) PPIE group at GSTT. This group has been pivotal in identifying research
priorities and informing this research proposal. In our first meeting in 2021, we discussed research priorities,
identifying that adults with PKU had concerns about the impact of PKU and its dietary management on long-term
review1, review2
health outcomes and functional ability in later life. This, along with my narrative scoping and my
nationwide survey of patients5, led to the development of the research questions. A recent PPIE meeting to revisit
the research proposal (based on feedback from my initial NIHR DCAF application), confirmed that this remains a
considerable research priority.
The research design evolved during multiple meetings with this PPIE group who informed the study design as
follows:
 Recruitment methods (suggesting screening and consent via video)
 Increasing test day attendance (offering compensation, written letters to request time off work and
provision of a suitable meal)
Test day assessment methods were r

secppi__001_b.text:
evised based on members’ feedback. The PPIE group raised

concerns about some participants feeling self-conscious about their body composition and functional
assessment results and provided suggestions to overcome this potential issue (see ‘Ethics’)
 Ideas of written communication for the feasibility study, including a fridge calendar and journal to track
progress, and drop-in video sessions for participants to discuss any questions about the diet and exercise
interventions
 Reviewing of Plain English Summary (PES)
The Research Support Service also reviewed and provided feedback, as a non-PKU audience, on the PES and
research proposal.
Wider adult PKU community
I conceived, designed, conducted, analysed and published a nationwide questionnaire survey to examine the
knowledge, perceptions and dietary behaviours of adults with PKU5. Findings confirmed the importance of this
research in adults with PKU, with >50% having concerns for their long-term health, that are currently being unmet.
Up to 75% of people with PKU considered research focused on the impact of the PKU diet on muscle and health
and how regimens can be tailored to body composition and physical activity status, as eit

secppi__001_c.text:
her extremely important
or very important, highlighting the value of this research.
Please describe the ways in which patients/service users, carers and the public will be actively involved in
the proposed research, including any training and support provided
Phenylketonuria PPIE group
I will continue my partnership with the Phenylketonuria (PKU) PPIE group at GSTT, who will be consulted regularly
throughout the fellowship.
This group brings lived experience, contributing valuable advice on engaging the PKU community to optimise
participation in research and ensuring that research meets the needs of this cohort.
The PKU PPIE group will be involved in, and advise on, the following during my fellowship:
 Advertising materials and methods to optimise recruitment
 Patient information sheet and consent forms to ensure clarity in the study and lay terminology so
participants can make an informed choice about participation
 Questionnaire and semi-structured interview design: questionnaires and interview questions designed by
the research team will be piloted with members of the PPIE group to test clarity and interpretation of
questions, readability, length and interview structure
 Dev

secppi__001_d.text:
eloping participant information resources for the feasibility study (eg. the exercise guide, fridge
calendar and the journal). Members will advise on the design, layout and wording used for these resources
 Dissemination methods for communicating this research to the PKU community and other IMD services
Planning for further research (eg. post-doctoral funding)

Project Advisory Group
Three PPIE members will join the Project Advisory Group to support discussions on study implementation,
recruitment, retention and any issues raised during the study (see ‘Research Plan’). A diverse PPIE group will be
selected to ensure members can represent the views of the wider PKU community.
Supporting, enabling and capturing participation
Meetings will be held online to enable involvement from a wide geographical area. Financial reimbursement for the
above consultations has been costed into this fellowship to compensate for PPIEs’ time and active involvement,
calculated using the NIHR payment guidance. New members to the group will be offered training in PPIE through
NIHR Biomedical Research Centre at GSTT (or similar) to ensure they feel equipped and confident to contribute to
these meetings. F

secppi__001_e.text:
urthermore, new members will be buddied with an established member for support and
mentoring.
The involvement and impact of the PPIE on this research and informing future research will be captured and
published utilising the GRIPP 2 reporting checklist54.
If it is considered not appropriate and meaningful to actively involve patients/service users, carers and the
public in your proposed research, please justify why
Not applicable

sectdars__001_a.text:
Proposed training and development programme
I have designed a training programme to develop both my academic and clinical skills in consultation with my
supervisors and using the Vitae Researcher Development Framework (see ‘Uploads’ for training timetable).
Formal study
The Department of Nutritional Sciences at King’s College London (KCL) fosters development of its doctoral
students through the Health Sciences Doctoral Training Centre (HSDTC) and the King’s Clinical Academic
Training Office (KCATO). Through the HSDTC and KCATO, I have selected courses in critical appraisal,
managing research data, academic writing, preparing for the upgrade, thesis writing, preparing for the viva, and
clinical academic career development courses.
Academic Training and Development
Research design and methods
Qualitative methods: I am keen to develop and strengthen my skills in qualitative research, especially for the
feasibility study and future research, and will attend the following courses offered by the Social Research
Association and National Centre for Social Research:
 Introduction to Qualitative Interviewing: This 1-day course introduces the method of qualitative interviewing
including effe
sectdars__001_a.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_b.text:
ctive interviewing, and key ethical and practical issues for consideration.
Introduction to Focus Groups: A 1-day course to develop skills in conducting focus groups.

 Questionnaire design: This 2-day course provides hands-on experience of designing questionnaires and
survey questions.
Clinical trials: I intend to advance the feasibility study to a full-scale trial in post-doctoral research, in addition to
leading further research in adults with IMDs, including complex interventions. I will attend two courses on clinical
trials:
 ‘Randomised Controlled Trials Course: A Guide to Design, Conduct, Analysis, Interpretation and
Reporting’ (University of Oxford). This 5-day course covers fundamental components of planning,
conducting and completing successful RCTs.
 ‘Developing and Evaluating Complex Interventions’ (University of Southampton): A 3-day course
structured around the MRC/NIHR Framework for developing and evaluating complex interventions.
Data analysis and interpretation (quantitative and qualitative)
To enhance my skills in data analysis and interpretation for both quantitative and qualitative research, I will attend
the following courses:
 ‘Practical Statistics for Me
sectdars__001_b.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_c.text:
dical Research’ (University College London): This 4-day course teaches
essential elements of research methodology and statistics, with a focus on understanding appropriate
methodology and interpretation of results.
 Advanced statistical analysis courses using SPSS and R (KCL), teaching the practical aspects of
performing more complex analysis using specific software (e.g. linear and logistic regression analyses for
my observational study).
 Qualitative Data Analysis (Social Research Association): A 1-day online course equipping researchers
with skills to undertake analysis of qualitative data.
Research Governance
I will attend Informed Consent Training / Principal Investigators Essentials / Good Clinical Practice training: These
courses are provided free by the NIHR and Biomedical Research Centre (BRC).
PPIE
I will undertake further PPIE training with attending courses provided by NIHR, including NIHR BRC Cambridge
PPIE online training sessions on facilitating successful PPIE groups, using PPIE to communicate research and
evaluating PPIE.
Dissemination
Nature Masterclass in Scientific Writing and Publishing: Through KCL, I will have access to these Masterclasses,
and plan to comp
sectdars__001_c.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_d.text:
lete training on scientific writing, publishing, effective communication in research, and peer
review.
Overseas visit
I will spend a week with Professor Luc van Loon (see ‘Collaborations’) at Maastricht University, to observe clinical
trials in protein metabolism, and to acquire knowledge in research design and advanced methods of investigating
protein metabolism.
Academic and clinical nexus
The following training will provide me with the skills to undertake the assessments included in my research
proposal, which will also be incorporated into my clinical practice, extending my scope of dietetic practice.
 Level 1 ISAK Anthropometry Course (St Mary’s University): 3-day course to develop my skills in
anthropometric profiling including skinfold analysis.
 Functional assessments: Under the supervision of Dr Oliver Witard, Exercise Physiologist, I will develop
my skills in conducting laboratory assessments of muscle functional capacity and establish their
application to clinical practice.
 Ultrasound: Dr Danielle Bear, Consultant Dietitian (GSTT), a former NIHR ICA awardee has extensive
experience in ultrasound techniques for assessing skeletal muscle mass, including publishing on t
sectdars__001_d.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_e.text:
his as
part of her NIHR fellowship. Under the guidance and supervision of Dr Bear, I will develop my skills in
conducting ultrasound assessments.
 Ionising Radiation (Medical Exposure) Regulations (IR(ME)R) certification: A 1-day course that is a pre-
requisite to undertaking DXA assessments.
 Phlebotomy skills 2-day course.
Clinical research integration visit: I will spend a week with Professor Anita MacDonald (see ‘Collaborations’) at
the Birmingham Children’s Hospital to observe research in IMD and how their research translates into clinical
practice, and to discuss dietary management priorities for patients with IMDs.
Leadership: Through the NIHR, I will have access to the Leadership e-learning modules. I plan to complete these
to develop and strengthen my leadership skills across both my academic and clinical roles.
Professional Training and Development
Clinical mentorship
As the lead Senior Specialist Dietitian, I will continue to provide regular mentorship to the Dietetic team. This
continuity in my involvement with the clinical team will allow me to maintain my clinical skills and translate my
research into clinical practice.
Conference attendance
Attending, and contribut
sectdars__001_e.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_f.text:
ing to, relevant national and international metabolic conferences will ensure my clinical
knowledge remains current.
Clinical practice
Care Innovation
Along with the professional development training outlined above, I will establish new care pathways for PKU
clinical assessment, adopting the skills gained in anthropometric and functional assessments. These assessment
techniques are novel in IMD and would allow for more individualised assessment and tailored dietary interventions.
Maintaining my involvement in these clinics will ensure that these developments translate into improved patient
care.
With the NHS advancing use of virtual patient consults, GSTT IMD service has adopted patient webinars to deliver
updates in clinical management. I will contribute to the development, updating and running of webinars and
provide specialist guidance for complex questions regarding dietary management of IMDs.
International Clinical Advisor
I will continue my role on the Advisory Board for the Global DARE Foundation including annual revision and
publication of dietary guidance, hosting training webinars, and providing ongoing clinical expertise to Dietitians
internationally.
Clinical educator
I
sectdars__001_f.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_g.text:
will continue to provide clinical education on the dietary management of IMD for dietetic students at KCL
(maximum 6-hours/year).
Primary Doctoral Supervisor
PRIMARY DOCTORAL SUPERVISOR 1
Name Professor Kevin Whelan
Institution King’s College London
Position Professor of Dietetics and Head of Department of Nutritional Sciences
Description of support provided
Professor Whelan will be my Primary Doctoral Supervisor. He is the inaugural Professor of Dietetics at King’s, and
a Fellow of the British Dietetic Association. He is a world expert in translational dietetic research who has led
numerous observational studies, feasibility studies and clinical trials to understand the application of nutrition to
improve clinical outcomes and quality of life in patients with chronic diseases, aligning with the focus of my
proposed research. He has expertise in data analysis (including teaching Statistics to Masters students) and will
provide me with statistical support, expertise that will be invaluable to my PhD fellowship.
Professor Whelan has published >220 full papers in peer-reviewed journals, including on body composition,
dietary intake and patient reported outcomes. He has received fundi
sectdars__001_g.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_h.text:
ng >£7.8 million, and in 2021 was a Clarivate
Highly Cited Researcher for multiple highly cited papers in the top 1% for field. Professor Whelan has supervised
16 PhDs to completion, including two previous and one current NIHR doctoral fellowships. Professor Whelan is a
committed educator with an MA in Higher Education Teaching and was awarded the King’s PhD Supervisory
Excellence Award 2021.
Professor Whelan is a HEE/NIHR Mentor, and former member of the HEE/NIHR ICA Clinical Lectureships Awards
Panel. He supervised my NIHR PCAF, therefore we have already collaborated on two research studies, and
published three papers together on PKU, protein and muscle. We have an established and positive working
relationship and Professor Whelan has contributed significantly to supporting my research training and developing
this proposal.
Additional Academic Supervisor(s)
ADDITIONAL ACADEMIC SUPERVISOR 1
Name Dr Oliver Witard
Institution King’s College London
Position Senior Lecturer in Exercise Metabolism & Nutrition
Description of support provided
Dr Oliver Witard will be my Academic Supervisor. His research explores the interaction of exercise and nutritional
interventions, primarily protein
sectdars__001_h.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_i.text:
nutrition, to offset the age-related decline in muscle mass and function. His
expertise is therefore directly aligned with my research proposal.
Dr Witard has supervised five PhD students to completion (three as primary and two as secondary supervisor),
and is currently supervising one PhD as primary, and another as secondary, supervisor. During my NIHR PCAF, I
collaborated with Dr Witard on several research activities, culminating in the publications of a narrative review and
scoping review on protein requirements and protein status in people with PKU. Dr Witard has been involved
throughout in developing this doctoral research proposal, providing vital knowledge and insight to the research
design and the proposed functional measurements.
Dr Witard has extensive experience in the management of human trials in protein nutrition and muscle metabolism
research, including the assessments of muscle functional capacity. He has led on multiple studies investigating
nutritional and exercise interventions in young and older adults with the primary outcome targeted at optimising
skeletal muscle mass and function. His expertise will be invaluable during my fellowship and by informing the
des
sectdars__001_i.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_j.text:
ign of further research.
Dr Witard will provide training and supervision in conducting the functional assessments as part of the
observational and feasibility studies. Under the supervision of Dr Witard, I will have access to state-of-the-art
Exercise Physiology laboratories in the Centre for Human and Applied Physiological Sciences where my research
will be conducted.
Clinical Supervisor(s)
PRACTICE SUPERVISOR 1
Name Dr Radha Ramachandran
Institution Guy’s and St Thomas’ NHS Foundation Trust
Position Service Lead and Consultant in Adult Inherited Metabolic Diseases
Description of support provided
Dr Radha Ramachandran will be my Practice and 3rd Academic Supervisor. She was appointed Consultant in
Metabolic Medicine in 2013. Dr Ramachandran chairs the UK Adult Inherited Metabolic Diseases (IMD)
Physicians’ Group and sits on the British IMD Group Clinical Guidelines and NICE Diagnostic Accuracy
Committees. She has international expertise in, and sits on Advisory Boards for, ORPHAN lipid and peroxisomal
disorders.
Dr Ramachandran completed an NIHR Doctoral Research Fellowship in 2011 during specialist training in
Chemical Pathology and Metabolic Medicine at Imperial College Healthca
sectdars__001_j.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_k.text:
re NHS Trust. Pursuing her research
interests, she has successfully supervised BSc and MSc projects, is Principal/Co-investigator for commercial
Phase 1, 2 and 3 trials for rare IMDs, and has supervised my NIHR PCAF.
I work closely with Dr Ramachandran to jointly manage IMD patients with complex nutritional needs. We interact
daily in multidisciplinary clinics, and in departmental service development and research meetings. She has
provided continued clinical supervision and, as Service Lead, opportunities to advance the dietetic service - both
vital for my professional development as Lead IMD Dietitian. We have collaborated on several audits, service
improvements and research projects, leading to a number of abstracts, international presentations and
publications.
Given the clinical mentorship she has provided thus far, our shared clinical and research interests and the need for
a multidisciplinary approach to managing patients with IMD, having Dr Ramachandran as my Practice and
Academic Supervisor will provide me with the support needed to further develop my career as a clinical academic
in IMD.
PRACTICE SUPERVISOR 2
Name Dr Danielle Bear
Guy’s and St Thomas’ NHS Foundation Trust
sectdars__001_k.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_l.text:
and King’s College
Institution
London
Consultant Dietitian – Critical Care (GSTT) and Honorary/Adjunct
Position
Lecturer (KCL)
Description of support provided
Dr Danielle Bear is a Consultant Dietitian recognised nationally and internationally for her expertise in the nutrition
management of critical ill patients. Dr Bear has been awarded British Dietetic Association Roll of Honour three
times, and is a past recipient of the Rose Simmonds and Elizabeth Washington awards.
Dr Bear established the critical care Dietetic service at GSTT and has extensive experience in leadership and
fostering professional development. Over the last seven years, Dr Bear has provided mentorship supporting my
professional development and research experience, and her ongoing support will be invaluable as I further
develop and expand my professional skillset.
Dr Bear embarked on her career as a clinical academic following completion of an NIHR CDRF in 2020. Her
doctoral research investigated the measurement and prevention of skeletal muscle wasting in critical illness. Dr
Bear has continued research exploring nutrition interventions, including protein requirements, to reduce muscle
wasting, and is an expert
sectdars__001_l.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_m.text:
in techniques to measure body composition in clinical practice. Her expertise is therefore
directly aligned with my research proposal, and will be invaluable during my fellowship and the implementation of
research findings into clinical practice.
Dr Bear bridges both clinical and academic settings in her role as a Consultant Dietitian at GSTT and honorary
lecture at KCL. She has supported numerous Dietitians to undertake further academic training and research,
including supervising MSc students. Dr Bear brings experience of developing a clinical academic career in
Dietetics, which will be instrumental in supporting me in developing my career.
Collaborations
In preparing this application, I established the following collaborations that I will continue to develop to support my
research and training.
Centre for Human and Applied Physiological Sciences, KCL
The Centre is committed to integrative and translational research focused on human physiological function and
adaptation in health and disease. Based on REF 2021, CHAPS is a leading UK centre (2nd in UK) with 75% of
publications ranked as world leading. Through my collaboration with Dr Witard (Supervisor), I will have access to
CHA
sectdars__001_m.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_n.text:
PS facilities and the physiologists/exercise scientists with expertise in muscle, ageing and exercise.
Developing collaborations with the scientists will strengthen the partnerships between KCL and the GSTT Adult
Metabolic service to support my doctoral, and future, research.
Physiotherapy Department, GSTT
Dr Jacky Jones, Deputy Chief AHP Research Lead and Head of Physiotherapy, and clinical physiotherapists at
GSTT, informed the proposal for the feasibility study exercise intervention. I will continue to collaborate with Dr
Jones and a physiotherapist during my fellowship on the design, implementation and analysis of the exercise
intervention. This will strengthen my partnership with the Physiotherapy Department, who will be integral to future
IMD research involving exercise interventions.
Professor Anita MacDonald, Consultant Dietitian, Birmingham Children’s Hospital.
Professor MacDonald is an international leader in the management of IMDs and has authored over 200
publications, many in PKU research. I worked with Professor MacDonald in a multi-centre study and visited her
team to observe clinical research in PKU. I will further develop this collaboration with Professor MacDonald
sectdars__001_n.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_o.text:
and
her research team during my fellowship. Professor MacDonald’s experience in translational research in PKU and
clinical expertise will be invaluable in fostering my development as a clinical academic. She is a clinical academic
that I would like to emulate, with my career ambition to be a Clinical Professor of Dietetics in IMDs.
International: Professor Luc van Loon, School of Nutrition and Translational Research in Metabolism,
Maastricht University Medical Centre+ (MUMC+)
Professor van Loon is the scientific coordinator of the Stable Isotope Research Centre at MUMC+ and has world-
leading expertise in protein metabolism research and tracer techniques. Establishing collaborations with Professor
van Loon and his research team will provide me with the opportunity to develop skills in the application of tracer
techniques to apply in future research investigating protein requirements for people with PKU.
Patient organisations
The National Society of PKU (NSPKU, patient support charity) has a long-standing involvement in PKU research
and a large patient and healthcare professional membership. I have developed collaborations with the NSPKU
through my clinical role and will continue t
sectdars__001_o.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_p.text:
o foster this partnership during my fellowship and post-award, providing
opportunities for me to engage with key stakeholders and service users from across the UK in research
development, participation and dissemination.
Industry: Arla Foods Ingredients (AFI)
Through Dr Witard’s (Supervisor) longstanding collaboration with AFI, I have developed links with the AFI nutrition
scientists. AFI has an interest in protein metabolism and protein substitute development for PKU, and has funded
multiple studies in this area. This collaboration will be important for further research collaborations and in
supporting the translation of the research outcomes into patient care. I plan to continue to strengthen this industry
collaboration during my fellowship and post-award.
Healthcare professional groups
I will continue my collaborations with clinicians and academics working in IMD through my involvement in national
and international IMD groups. Outcomes from my doctoral research will influence patient care and management
guidelines, and therefore, collaborations with these group will be important to disseminate findings, contribute to
guidelines and collaborate on future research.
Host Organisati
sectdars__001_p.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_q.text:
on Support Statement
The following statement of support should be composed jointly by the Heads of Department / Senior Managers of
the employing host organisation and the partner organisation.
The Heads of Department / Senior Managers of these organisations will be able to add the statement of support to
the form once they have confirmed participation. Invitations to participate are sent by the applicant via the
'Participants and Signatories' section of the form.
Statement of support
Sarah Firman plans to complete her ICA fellowship at King’s College London (KCL, Host Organisation) and Guy’s
& St Thomas’ NHS Foundation Trust (GSTT, Partner Organisation). Both are founding institutions of the King’s
Health Partners (KHP) Academic Health Sciences Centre, whose aim is to unite world-class research, education
and clinical practice and translate this into excellent patient care, therefore providing the perfect organisational
support for Sarah’s Fellowship.
Sarah is the Lead Senior Dietitian in the Inherited Metabolic Diseases service at GSTT and has established the
service over the last seven years. During this time, she has demonstrated drive to enhance patient care through
research, d
sectdars__001_q.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_r.text:
evelopment of care pathways and extending scope of dietetic practice. Sarah was successfully
awarded an NIHR PCAF in 2019, and presented her PCAF research at conferences, it was discussed in national
meetings, and she has published all four outputs from it. There are existing, supportive and productive research
collaborations between Sarah and all her academic and clinical supervisors that will be strengthened during this
DCAF.
The Department of Nutritional Sciences at KCL was the first university department of its kind in Europe and is
recognised as a world-leader in Nutrition and Dietetics research. In REF 2021, the Department was returned under
Unit of Assessment 3 (Allied Health Professions, Dentistry, Nursing and Pharmacy) where 70% of research was 4*
(world-leading) and 95% of research was 4* and 3* (world-leading or internationally excellent). This resulted in the
highest ranking in the UK for UoA3 for research, research output, research GPA and research power. Importantly
for Sarah’s PhD, 100% of the research environment was rated 4* (world-leading) and KCL has one of the highest
PhD completion rates in the UK.
The Department of Nutritional Sciences at KCL has a well-develo
sectdars__001_r.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_s.text:
ped infrastructure to support and monitor the
development of postgraduate researchers. Sarah will have access to the largest collection of nutrition journals in
the UK. She will be provided with two academic supervisors, a combined academic & clinical supervisor, and
another clinical supervisor, a thesis progression committee, an induction programme, regular monitoring of
progress, and development of research skills specific to her fellowship. There is a well-equipped Metabolic
Research Unit for clinical research visits, the Centre for Human and Applied Physiological Sciences at KCL for
function assessments, and our Statistical Support Unit for Sarah’s academic training and development throughout
her DCAF.
GSTT demonstrates a commitment to progressive academic study and supporting clinical academic roles for Allied
Health Professionals (AHPs). Dr Jacky Jones has just been appointed as the first AHP Research Lead for the
organisation and has a position on the Trust R&D board. The Trust has recently published their AHP strategy with
research as a stated priority, including supporting clinical academic careers, hosting of clinical academic posts and
commitment to supporting clinical a
sectdars__001_s.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_t.text:
cademic training.
The Department of Nutrition and Dietetics at GSTT is the largest Dietetic Department in the UK with dietetic
leaders in several areas of clinical care, and extensive experience supporting AHP clinical researchers. Our Trust
has four Consultant Dietitians (the largest number of any Trust in the UK) all with PhDs (including two funded by
NIHR awards), and a number of other staff with PhDs and MRes.
There is a strong history of successful support of clinical academic careers among dietitians across the
departments at King’s Health Partners (including KCL and GSTT).
There are currently three joint-funded permanent clinical academic dietitians (the largest number of Clinical
Academic Dietitians at any HEI in the UK) who lead clinical services at GSTT and lead research at KCL. There
have been two former HEE/NIHR Lectureships at King’s Health Partners both of whom successfully completed
projects and moved to clinical academic positions elsewhere. There are three previous and one current recipient of
HEE/NIHR ICA Doctoral Fellowships all of whom successfully completed their PhDs on time and are now in
Clinical Academic positions. Across King’s Health Partners, our combine
sectdars__001_t.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_u.text:
d departments represent the largest group
of academic, research and clinical academic dietitians in the UK.
Creating an inclusive environment that puts colleagues, researchers and patients first, and which promotes
equality, diversity, and inclusion (EDI) are key priorities for both KCL and GSTT. In 2021, KCL were awarded the
institutional Athena Swan Silver accreditation recognising their work to advance gender equality. The Faculty,
School and Department in which Sarah will be based have EDI Leads who report into College council, as well as
an EDI forum for students and staff. GSTT has an incredibly diverse workforce and takes proactive measures at all
levels to make EDI a part of everyday business. Further details of approaches to EDI are available from KCL and
GSTT.
Sarah has chosen to be seconded to KCL and retain substantive employment with GSTT for the duration of her
fellowship. This will provide Sarah with the opportunity to be integrated into the Department of Nutritional Sciences
at KCL, where she will undertake her research, working alongside over 20 doctoral students, while remaining
connected to the clinical/patient setting. The Department of Nutrition and Dietetics a
sectdars__001_u.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_v.text:
t GSTT supported Sarah by
backfilling her post during her HEE/NIHR PCAF, and will do so again should she be successful.
Following her PhD, we are committed to supporting Sarah in her ambition to become a leading clinical academic in
the dietary management of Inherited Metabolic Disorders and we will work together to build a business case for a
joint clinical academic appointment. Of note, all of our previous dietitians who received an HEE/NIHR ICA Doctoral
Fellowship were subsequently appointed as Consultant Dietitians at GSTT and Clinical Academics at KCL through
permanent joint funding, and we anticipate the same for Sarah.
We emphatically support Sarah in this doctoral fellowship application and in her developing an integrated clinical
academic career.
Rhys White MRes RD, Guy’s & St Thomas’ NHS Foundation Trust
Professor Kevin Whelan PhD RD FBDA, King’s College London

Justification of Cost: This is to cover the cost of the online

secsb__001_w.text:
electronic data capturing system, REDCap, which will
be used to ensure data is accurately captured and to allow for more efficient data analysis. REDCap also includes
online survey features which will be used for the feasibility study. REDCap is a secure web application for building
and managing online surveys and databases, which is approved by KCL and GSTT, being currently used across
both sites. There are self-training videos available on setting up, building and managing the database. In addition,
support for trouble shooting is available through KCL/GSTT. There are further plans to link REDCap to EPIC, the
electronic patient management system at GSTT, allowing for patient data to be directly transferred from EPIC to
REDCap. There is an annual cost of £2,500 per year to have access to REDCap.
